title.en: How to File an RTI Application — Format, ₹10 Fee, and the 30-Day Clock

answerBox.en: Any citizen can file an RTI application to any public authority: write your questions, pay the ₹10 fee, and address it to the department's Public Information Officer — online at rtionline.gov.in for central departments, or by post/state portals for state bodies. The PIO must reply within 30 days (48 hours for life-and-liberty matters). First appeal is free; second appeal goes to the Information Commission.

en.label: First appeal
en.startsFrom: from reply/deadline lapse; to senior officer

en.title: Frame questions that must be answered
en.summary: Ask for records and specifics, not opinions.
en.detail: RTI (Right to Information Act, 2005) fetches **information held in records**: file notings, status of your application, copies of documents, reasons recorded, action taken on a complaint, expenditure details. Ask numbered, specific questions ("provide the current status and file noting on application no. X dated Y"). PIOs cannot answer "why is the department inefficient" — they must supply records. Exemptions (s.8 — security, privacy of others, cabinet papers) exist but are narrow and the PIO must justify invoking them.

en.title: File it: online, post, or in person
en.summary: rtionline.gov.in for the centre; state portals or post for states.
en.detail: Central ministries/departments: **[rtionline.gov.in](https://rtionline.gov.in)** — pay the **₹10 fee** online and track digitally. State bodies: the state RTI portal where one exists, or **registered post** to "The Public Information Officer, [department]" with a ₹10 IPO/court-fee stamp/DD per state rules. **BPL applicants pay nothing** (attach proof). No reasons need be given for seeking information — the Act forbids asking why. Our ₹199 service drafts the application with legally-tight questions.

en.title: The 30-day clock and what replies must contain
en.summary: Information, or a justified refusal — silence is a deemed refusal.
en.detail: The PIO must respond within **30 days** (35 via assistant PIO; **48 hours** where life or liberty is at stake). Copying charges (₹2/page) may be demanded — the clock pauses till you pay. A refusal must cite the exact exemption; **silence beyond 30 days is a deemed refusal** and the information must then be provided free. Misaddressed applications must be transferred to the right authority within 5 days (s.6(3)) — so a wrong guess still works.

en.title: Appeals: free, and they bite
en.summary: First appeal in-house; second to the Information Commission.
en.detail: No/bad reply? File the **first appeal within 30 days** to the department's First Appellate Authority (free; cite the application and the failure). Still stuck: **second appeal within 90 days** to the Central/State **Information Commission** — which can order disclosure and impose **penalties up to ₹25,000 on the PIO personally** (₹250/day of delay). That personal penalty is why RTI appeals get answered. Persistent stonewalling on public-interest matters can also go to the High Court.

rows.label: Copying charges
rows.note: A4; inspection of records ₹5/hour after first hour

rows.label: First + second appeals
rows.note: No fee at either appellate stage (most states)

en.footnote: RTI applies to public authorities — government departments, PSUs, and substantially government-funded bodies. Private companies are outside it directly, but information about them held by regulators is fair game.

en.q: Can the department ask why I want the information?
en.a: No — Section 6(2) expressly bars asking for reasons. You need only your contact details and the fee. Any PIO demanding justification is violating the Act; note it in your first appeal.

en.q: What if I sent the RTI to the wrong department?
en.a: Section 6(3) obliges the receiving authority to transfer it to the correct one within 5 days and inform you. Your 30-day clock runs from the transferee's receipt. So file your best guess rather than not filing.

Section 8 exemptions: national security, trade secrets and commercial confidence, personal information with no public-interest nexus, cabinet papers pending decision, and information barred by courts. Even then, severability applies — the non-exempt part must be given — and the public-interest override can defeat several exemptions.

en.q: The PIO simply never replied. What now?
en.a: That is a deemed refusal: file the first appeal citing the lapse — information ordered after a deemed refusal comes free of copying charges. If the first appeal also fails, the second appeal puts the PIO at risk of a personal penalty up to ₹25,000, which reliably concentrates minds.

en.q: Can RTI help my stuck personal file (pension, certificate, complaint)?
en.a: It is the classic use: ask for the current status, the officer holding the file, file notings, and the prescribed timeline for disposal. Files move when inaction becomes a documented record with names attached — many stuck matters resolve within the 30-day RTI window itself.

reviewer: Adv. Arjun Nair, Bar Council of Kerala, KER/0973/2012
author: WakilBhai Editorial Team
